_status: HELD-INACTIVE 2026-07-13: thin calibration (no published optimal band; danger anchor would be NWS flood stage). too_low/low ingested; complete + activate when an outfitter optimal/danger key exists.
name: Kings River
region: Ozark Mountains / Northwest Arkansas (Carroll & Madison counties)
difficultyRating: Class I (mostly I-I+) on the ~82-mi lower river; advanced Class III+ whitewater only on the upper 11 mi (Dripping Springs-Hwy 74)
description: The Kings River is a free-flowing, undammed Ozark stream that runs generally north for about 90-93 miles from its headwaters in the Boston Mountains of Madison County, Arkansas (about 1.5 mi east of Boston) to the White River arm of Table Rock Lake at the Arkansas-Missouri state line. Its uppermost 11 miles (Dripping Springs to Hwy 74) form a turbulent Class III+ whitewater run with a waterfall for advanced paddlers only; the remaining ~82 miles are a gentle, scenic Class I float past tall bluffs, gravel bars, and clear pools, popular for smallmouth-bass fishing and family canoe/kayak trips. Because it is rain-fed and flashy, the Kings floats best from late winter through June and typically drops below floatable levels by mid-summer, though its lowest, lake-influenced reach holds water into August. Arkansas has designated it an Extraordinary Resource Waterbody.
managingAuthority: Free-flowing/undammed with no single manager. Public floating access at road crossings plus private/fee accesses via outfitters (Kings River Outfitters and Trigger Gap Outfitters, both near Berryville/Eureka Springs). The Nature Conservancy owns riverfront land (Kings River Preserve / Rockhouse area). Arkansas Game & Fish Commission (fisheries). Arkansas Dept. of Energy & Environment / Division of Environmental Quality administers the 'Extraordinary Resource Waterbody' designation. Downstream mouth (Table Rock Lake) is managed by the USACE Little Rock District. Streamgauge 07050500 operated by the USGS.
accessLaw: Arkansas: the public has the right to float and wade streams that are legally navigable and to use the water/streambed below the ordinary high-water mark, but banks and bordering land (including gravel bars above the high-water mark) are largely private -- entry/exit is generally limited to public road rights-of-way and designated (often private, fee) accesses. Do not trespass on private banks. The Kings is a state-designated Extraordinary Resource Waterbody with added protections against streambed alteration and pollution. Note: access is specifically NOT recommended at the US-412 bridge east of Marble.

sections.name: Upper Kings: Dripping Springs to Hwy 74 (advanced whitewater)
sections.description: Turbulent Class III+ whitewater with a waterfall; advanced paddlers only, NOT a casual float. Berryville gauge (07050500) is far downstream and only a rough proxy for this reach; it needs high spring flows. American Whitewater reach id 2637.

sections.name: Marble to Marshall Ford
sections.description: Upper floatable reach; ~7 hrs. Put in at the Marble access -- Arkansas tourism advises AGAINST access at the US-412 bridge east of Marble. Includes a low-water bridge portage.

sections.name: Marshall Ford to Rockhouse
sections.description: Long, remote reach (~8 hrs); good for overnight camping. Trigger Gap Outfitters lists it at ~15.7 mi.

sections.name: Rockhouse to Trigger Gap
sections.description: Most popular day float (~4 hrs). Runs through The Nature Conservancy land with rock features, gravel bars, and deep swimming holes; low-water bridge portage. Some outfitters list ~7.7 mi.

sections.name: Trigger Gap to Hwy 62 (JD Fletcher)
sections.description: Trophy smallmouth-bass section (18-inch minimum keeper); ~7 hrs, can be run as an overnight. HAZARD: low-water bridge near the end with its center blasted out -- outfitters advise going straight down the middle or portaging on the left. Some outfitters list ~12.9 mi.

sections.name: Hwy 62 to MO 86 (into Table Rock Lake)
sections.description: Longest reach (8-12 hrs); enters the White River arm of Table Rock Lake at the AR/MO line -- expect slack backwater, motorboat traffic, and wind. Floatable into August thanks to lake influence.

takeOut.name: Missouri Hwy 86 / White River arm of Table Rock Lake

research.calibrationKeySource: Kings River Outfitters / Trigger Gap published minimum-floatable levels (too_low/low only); HELD inactive — no published optimal band and the only danger anchor is the NWS flood stage
research.calibrationNotes: GAUGE CORRECTION (safety-critical): the originally supplied ID 07050152 is 'Roaring River at Roaring River State Park', Barry County, MISSOURI (drainage 35.7 sq mi; 36.5810, -93.8337) -- a DIFFERENT river ~35 mi away, reading 84.7 cfs / 0.62 ft on 2026-07-13, which coincidentally matched the '~85 cfs / 0.62 ft' in the brief. The correct Kings River gauge is USGS 07050500 'Kings River near Berryville, AR' (Carroll County; drainage 527 sq mi; 36.4272, -93.6208), reading 199 cfs / 4.26 ft at 12:15 CDT on 2026-07-13 and rising on rain. Do NOT use 07050152 for the Kings. UNIT: gage height feet (USGS 00065) on 07050500; outfitter minimums (3.2/3.5 ft) and NWS flood stages (30/31/33/35 ft) share this NAVD88 963.53 ft datum, so the ladder is internally consistent. FLOATABILITY: minimum floatable 3.2 ft for the popular lower sections (Rockhouse->Trigger Gap->Hwy 62), 3.5 ft for the upper sections (Marble->Marshall Ford->Rockhouse). Best season late winter->June; the lowest, lake-influenced reach (Hwy 62->MO 86) holds water into August. The river is usually TOO LOW by July (Arkansas Canoe Club: 'the Kings is not runnable from Marshall down by June much less July'); despite that, a rain pulse has it floatable today. STAGE<->DISCHARGE reference points for future calibration (approx, gauge 07050500): ~83-89 cfs ~= 3.76 ft (summer baseflow, 'drier than normal'); 199 cfs ~= 4.26 ft (today); minimum-float 3.2 ft ~= roughly 50 cfs (unverified estimate). MIDDLE OF THE LADDER (optimal_min/optimal_max/high) is intentionally null -- no authoritative published thresholds exist and the flashy nature makes any single 'safe upper' number unreliable.
hazards: Low-water bridges must be portaged. One near the end of the Trigger Gap->US-62 section has its center blasted out; outfitters advise running straight down the middle or portaging on the left. Never run a submerged/flooded low-water bridge.

Flash flooding: this rain-fed Ozark stream is very flashy and can rise many feet within hours after upstream rain; levels change dramatically. Check the 07050500 gauge and the forecast before and during trips.
Strainers, logjams, and sweepers: the undammed river collects fallen trees and deadfall (especially after high water) that can pin boats.
Bony/low water: by mid-summer (often July onward) the river commonly drops below the 3.2-3.5 ft minimum floatable level; expect dragging and portaging over gravel bars.
Upper Kings (Dripping Springs->Hwy 74) is Class III+ whitewater with a waterfall -- advanced paddlers only, and poorly represented by the downstream Berryville gauge.
Table Rock Lake backwater on the lowest reach (US-62->MO-86): slack water, motorboat traffic, and wind extend the paddle.
Access is not recommended at the US-412 bridge east of Marble.
